Facts.text: Athanasius returns to Alexandria
Thomas, the first known Bishop of Finland, is granted resignation after having confessed to torture and forgery
The Prussian Confederation is formed
Spanish garrison of Middelburg, Netherlands, surrenders
Boris Godunov crowned Tsar of Russia
Michael Romanov, son of the Patriarch of Moscow, is elected the first Russian Tsar of the House of Romanov
Michiel de Ruyter appointed lt-admiral-general of Dutch fleet
British ambassador Joseph Yorke demands dismissal of Governor John de Graaff for saluting US flag
US Congress resolves establishment of a US mint
US Congress passes Presidential Succession Act
Freedom of worship is established in France under the constitution
Trinidad, West Indies, a Spanish colony, surrenders to the British
World’s first steam locomotive, built by Richard Trevithick, runs for the first time along the tramway of the Penydarren Ironworks in Merthyr Tydfil, Wales
Russia invades Finland, then part of the Swedish Kingdom, with 24,000 troops
Greek War of Independence against the Ottoman Empire begins
1st American Indian newspaper in US, "Cherokee Phoenix", published
1st known sewing machine patented in US by John Greenough of Washington, D.C.
1st US woman telegrapher is Sarah G. Bagley of Lowell, Massachusetts
US authorizes minting of $3 gold pieces
Congress outlaws foreign currency as legal tender in US
US issues flying eagle cents
Edwin T. Holmes installs 1st electric burglar alarm (Boston, Massachusetts)
Navajo Indians elect Herrero Grande as chief
Steeple of Chichester Cathedral collapses despite the desperate efforts of 70 workmen
US Confederate Constitution and presidency come into effect, last until the end of the Civil War in 1865
-22] Battle at Okolona, Mississippi
1st US Catholic parish church for black worshippers dedicated in Baltimore
Lucy B. Hobbs (Taylor) becomes 1st US woman to earn a DDS degree
Oakland Daily Tribune begins publication in Oakland, California
Telegraph manager George Coy of New Haven, Connecticut, issues the world's first telephone directory, a single page containing information for 50 subscribers
NYC's 24 hour race begins, winner with most mileage in 24 hours
Second French government of Jules Ferry begins
Washington Monument dedicated in Washington, D.C.
First US bacteriology laboratory opens in Brooklyn
Oregon becomes the first US state to make Labor Day a holiday
Constitutional delegates in Cuba adopt a constitution similar to that of the US
Dr. Harvey Cushing, 1st US brain surgeon, performs his 1st brain operation
Cornerstone laid for US army war college, Washington, D.C.
National Ski Association forms in Ishpeming, Michigan
SS Berlin sinks off the Hook of Holland (142 dead)
John Galsworthy's "Strife" premieres in London
John Galsworthy's play "Justice" premieres in London, aimned at highlighting conditions in prisons
Bai Lang ("White Wolf") troops attack Zhanjiang, China

In a secret meeting of civil and military leaders, Russian Foreign Minister Sazonov convinces them to support a plan for seizing the straits, controlled by Turkey, that block access to the Mediterranean
Russian 20th Army corps surrenders to the German 10th Army after being surrounded
Battle of Verdun begins with a German offensive, leads to an estimated 1 million casualties, and becomes the longest battle of the entire war (9 months)
British troopship SS Mendi sinks off Isle of Wight, 646 die
Australians chase Turkish troop out of Jericho, Dutch Palestine
The last Carolina parakeet dies in captivity at the Cincinnati Zoo (Cincinnati, Ohio)
German National Meeting accepts Anschluss: incorporation of Austria
Kurt Eisner, Premier of the Bavarian Republic, is assassinated by a far-right German nationalist in Munich
Revolutionary strike in Barcelona
Constituent Assembly of the Democratic Republic of Georgia adopts the country's first constitution
The London Conference on the Near East begins: the issue is the Allies' 1920 Treaty of Sevres, which had given part of Turkish Asia Minor to Greece
Airship "Rome" explodes at Hampton Roads, Virginia; 34 die
WHK-AM in Cleveland, Ohio begins radio transmissions
Andre Charlot's musical revue "Rats" opens at the Vaudeville Theatre, in London's West End
1st issue of "New Yorker" magazine published
Mass meeting of SPD's Reichsbanner Black-Red-Gold in Magdeburg
Alka Seltzer introduced
Chicago White Sox and NY Giants play the first exhibition night game
Andre Tardieu becomes Prime Minister of France, for the third time
US camera exposure meter patented to W.N. Goodwin
Nicaraguan patriot Augusto César Sandino and three others kidnapped and assassinated by the National Guard in Larreynaga
The League of Nations bans foreign national "volunteers" in the Spanish Civil War
Walter Waterman's Arrowbile, the 1st working automobile-airplane combination, tested at Santa Monica, California
Belgian government of Pierlot forms
Dutch RC bishops protest against persecution of Jews
"War As It Happens" news show premieres on NBC-TV, in New York City only

Battle of Monte Castello (Italy): Allied forces, including the first land battle of the Brazilian Expeditionary Force, defeat Germans after three months of fierce fighting in the foothills of the Apennine Mountains
Dutch Archbishop of Utrecht Johannes de Jong calls to the Allies for help with war casualties following the Nazi occupation of Netherlands
Operation Veritable: British Army captures Goch from Germany, forcing German retreat away from the Rhineland
WWII: US Army 10th Armored Division overthrows Germany's Orscholz line of defense
Anti-British demonstrations in Egypt
Whipper Billy Watsonbeats Bill Longson, to become wrestling champ
NASCAR is incorporated
US TV soap opera "A Woman to Remember" is 1st broadcast on DuMont Television Network
WOI TV channel 5 in Ames-Des Moines, IA (ABC/PBS) begins broadcasting
South Carolina House of Representatives urges "Shoeless Joe" Jackson be reinstated by Major League Baseball
Bangladesh Martyrs Day (martyrs of Bengali Language Movement)
Longest collegiate basketball game (6 OTs) Niagara beats Siena 88-81
Dodgers (Fort Worth) & Cubs (LA) "trade" minor league franchises
British artist Gerald Holtom designs Nuclear Disarmament logo, based on blended semaphore signals for the letters N and D; it later became an international peace symbol
Egypt-Syria as United Arab Republic elect Nasser president (99.9% vote)
Biathlon debuts at the Squaw Valley Winter Olympics; Klas Lestander from Sweden becomes the first Olympic champion ahead of Finland's Antti Tyrväinen and Soviet Aleksandr Privalov
Gabon adopts constitution
Mercury-Atlas 2 reentry Test reaches 172 km
Minister De Pous confirms natural gas reserves in Groningen, Netherlands
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
UK flies 24,000 rolls of Beatles wallpaper to US
150,000 demonstrate against leftist students in West Berlin
Baseball announces a minimum annual salary of $10,000
1st launching of heavy N-1 rocket at Baikonur Kazakhstan (explodes)
Jackson 5 make TV debut on "American Bandstand"
Pathet Lao conquers Xieng Khuang & Muong Suy
Series of tornadoes cuts through Mississippi & Louisiana killing 117
The Convention on Psychotropic Substances is signed at Vienna
Michael Weller's "Moonchildren" premieres in NYC
The first session of the Widgery Tribunal, investigating the events of 'Bloody Sunday' (30 January 1972), is held in Coleraine, County Derry
Chicago Black Hawks, record 262nd NHL game without being shut-out
Israeli fighter jets shoot down Libyan commercial airliner that strayed into restricted airspace over occupied Sinai Penninsula, killing 108
Israeli forces leave western Suez
Silver hits a record $5.96½ an ounce in London
Yugoslavia adopts constitution
Cardinal Willebrands installed as Archbishop of Utrecht
74 Unification Church couples wed in NYC
Electrical workers accidentally discover the Aztec Templo Mayor, or High Temple, two blocks from Mexico City's central square, the Zócalo
2 Iowa girls HS basketball teams play 4 scoreless quarters game was won 4-2 in 4th overtime period
Japan launches Hakucho X-ray satellite & Corsa-B (550/580 km)
British figure skater Robin Cousins wins men's singles gold medal at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics; goes on to pro career, TV analysis and stage performance & production
East German cross country skier Barbara Petzold wins her second gold medal of the Lake Placid Winter Olympics as part of the women's 4 x 5k relay team; also wins 10k gold
Liechtenstein becomes the smallest country to produce an Olympic champion as Hanni Wenzel wins the women's giant slalom at the Lake Placid Winter Games
Charles Rocket clearly says "f*ck" on "Saturday Night Live"
Japan launches Hinotori satellite to study solar flares (580/640 k)
NASA launches Comstar D-4
Donald Davis runs 1 mile backwards in 6 m 7.1 s
NBA San Diego Clippers begin a 29 game road losing streak
Evert van Benthem wins his 13th Elfstedentocht, a long distance 11 city skating race
Largest NBA crowd to date 44,970 (Atlanta at Detroit)
AIDS patient Ryan White returns to classes at Western Middle School
Syrian army marches into Beirut
Gustafson skates world record 10km (13:48.20)
Televangelist Jimmy Swaggert confesses his sins to his congregation
US bust Chinese drug smuggling ring, capturing a record 820 lbs of heroin worth $1 billion at street value
USSR announces that Iraq has agreed to a proposal to end the Gulf War, but the US calls the plan unacceptable
Sergei Bubka pole vaults world record indoor (6.15 m)
CFL's Sacramento Gold Miners become San Antonio Texans
RAF pilot Jo Salter is the first woman to fly a Tornado fighter jet
Soyuz TM-23 launches into orbit
"Empire Strikes Back, special edition" premieres
NASA's STS 82 (Space Shuttle Discovery 22) lands
43rd Grammy Awards: "Beautiful Day"; Shelby Lynne win
Canadian world champion women's ice hockey team wins first of 4 consecutive Winter Olympic gold medals with a 3-2 win over the United States at Salt Lake City
The first European political party organization, the European Greens, is established in Rome
Italian Prime Minister Romano Prodi resigns from office; his resignation is rejected by the President of Italy, Giorgio Napolitano
"Claybourne Park" by Bruce Norris opens off-Broadway at Playwrights Horizons in New York (1st play to win a Tony and a Pulitzer)
Thailand's GDP rose 3.8% in the fourth quarter of 2010 after a minor recession caused by GDP contractions of 0.4% in the second quarter and 0.3% in the third quarter of 2010, it is reported today
Yemeni voters go to the polls for a presidential election where the only candidate on the ballot paper is vice-president Abd Rabbuh Mansur al-Hadi
21 people are killed and 54 are injured in a bombing in Hyderabad, India
83 people are killed and 250 are injured in a series of bombing attacks in Damascus, Syria
10 words from the 15th C Voynich manuscript have been decoded
11 people are killed after a Libyan Air Force plan crashes in Tunisia
Canada take the curling double at the Sochi Winter Olympics beating Great Britain, 9-3 in the final; also win women's gold medal
Park Seung-hi wins Olympic women's 1,000m short track speed skating gold medal in Sochi for her 2nd gold medal of the Games; 3,000m relay winner
Police in Kiev open fire on protesters: 27 killed and around 570 injured
Viktor An of Russia wins 6th career Olympic short track speed skating gold medal as part of the 5,000m relay team in Sochi; 2nd gold of the day (500m) and 3rd of Games (1,000m)
58th Daytona 500: Denny Hamlin wins closest finish in race history - by just 0.01s from Martin Truex Jr
Bombings in the Syrian cities of Homs and Damascus kill 140 people, Islamic State claims responsibility
Bodies of 87 African migrants wash ashore at Zawiya, Libya
Plane crashes into shopping centre in Essendon, Melbourne, Australia killing the five people on board
Indian film star Kamal Haasan launches a new political party - Makkal Needhi Mayyam in Tamil Nadu state

Norwegian cross country skiers Martin Johnsrud Sundby, Johannes Høsflot & Klæbo win their 2nd Olympic gold medals in Pyeongchang in the men's team sprint freestyle; trio also wins gold in 4 x 10k relay
Winter Olympics: Jessie Diggins and Kikkan Randall become the first Americans in history to win a cross country gold medal (team sprint)
Japanese spacecraft Hayabusa-2 touches down on asteroid Ryugu on mission to collect rock samples
The Lunar Library, a 30 million page digital library launched on board Israel’s Beresheet Lunar Lander, aiming to be stored on the Moon
First democratic transfer of power in Niger, the world's poorest country, as former interior minister Mohamed Bazoum wins Presidential election
WHO pleads with Tanzania to start reporting COVID-19 cases amid fears of a hidden epidemic in the country
60 people die in an explosion at an unregulated gold mine near Gaoua, Burkina Faso
Australia's international border reopens to vaccinated tourists after 704 days, nearly two years
Colombia's Constitutional Court, its highest court, decriminalizes abortion
Pope Alexander III canonizesThomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury
Joan of Arc's first day of interrogation during her trial for heresy
KingPhilip IIadvises Cardinal Antoine Perrenot de Granvelle to retire from the Netherlands to Franche-Comté in France

Treaty of Adrianople: Holy Roman EmperorMaximilian IIand Ottoman SultanSelim IIsign peace agreement between the Habsburg monarchy and the Ottoman Empire; the Ottomans receive cash and territories of Transylvania, Moldavia, and Wallachia
Prince William of Orangeis appointed Stadtholder of Gelderland
Jacobite Rising 1745: British forces surrender Inverness Castle toBonnie Prince Charlieand the Jacobite forces
English House of Commons triesJohn Wilkesin absentia and finds him guilty of publishing a seditious libel for his "Essay on Women," an obscene parody ofAlexander Pope’s "Essay on Man"
Karl MarxandFriedrich Engelspublish "The Communist Manifesto" in London
George Bancroftbecomes the American Geographical Society's first president
Texas Rangers win Confederate victory atBattle of Valverde, Confederate Arizona (now New Mexico)
Benjamin DisraelisucceedsWilliam Gladstoneas British Prime Minister
North Caroline Legislature adjourns for the day to mark the passing ofFrederick Douglass
Gustav Mahlerconducts his last concerto, Berceuse élégiaque, in New York City
Irish Nationalist Eamon De Valera calls a convention of the Sinn Féin, declaring the Republican Government the only legitimate one in all Ireland
Marc Connelly's play "Green Pastures" premieres in NYC
US officerOmar Bradleyis promoted to the rank of brigadier general
First instant developing camera, the Land Camera Model 95, is demonstrated byEdwin Landat the Optical Society of America in NYC
Friedrich Dürrenmatt's play "Die Physiker (The Physicists)" premieres in Zurich
Civil rights activist and Muslim ministerMalcolm Xis shot dead by Nation of Islam followers at Audubon Ballroom in New York City
IndonesianPresident Sukarnodismisses General Nasution
Apple Records releases Mary Hopkin's debut album "Post Card", in the UK,  produced byPaul McCartney
Ted Williamssigns 5-year contract to manage Washington Senators
Watergate figures John Mitchell,H. R. Haldeman& John D. Ehrlichman sentenced to 2½-8 yrs for conspiracy and obstruction of justice
American country singer-songwriterDolly Partontops the US pop chart with her single "9 to 5" from the film of the same name, in which she co-starred with Lily Tomlin and Jane Fonda
American speed skaterEric Heidenwins 1,500m in Olympic record 1:55.44; 4th of record 5 gold medal sweep of events at Lake Placid
National League baseball playerTim Rainesis awarded a $1.2 million salary for 1985 by arbitrator
Pete Rosemeets with MLB CommissionerPeter Ueberrothto discuss his gambling
"The Batman Theme" byDanny Elfmanwins Grammy Award for Best Instrumental Composition at 32nd Annual Grammy Awards
32nd Grammy Awards:Bette Midler's "Wind Beneath My Wings" (both Record of the Year and Song of the Year), and Bonnie Raitt's "Nick of Time" (Album of the Year) win
Kristi Yamaguchiof US wins Olympic gold medal in women's figure skating
Lahore Declaration signed between India'sAtal Bihari Vajpayeeand Pakistan's Navaz Sharif on use of nuclear weapons
Bill Maher's political talk show "Real Time with Bill Maher" debuts on HBO
US PresidentBarack Obamameets with theDalai Lama
Alex Pucciocompletes the first female ascent of Nagual V13 (8B) at Hueco Tanks

India's BJP party issues resolution country had "defeated COVID under the able, sensitive, committed and visionary leadership of Prime MinisterNarendra Modi”, massive second wave hits two months later
Russian leaderVladimir Putinrecognises Russia-backed separatists in two Ukrainian regions, ordering in troops for "peacekeeping functions"
US PresidentJoe Bidenvows unwavering support for Ukraine in a speech from Warsaw Castle, Poland, a day after he made a surprise trip to Ukraine
US PresidentDonald Trumpfires US Air Force Chief of Staff General Charles Q. Brown as Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff as part of a Pentagon shake up
Groningen, Netherlands, begins using Gregorian calendar
Richard Nixon becomes the first US President to visit China, normalizing relations between the countries in a meeting with Chinese leaderMao Zedongin Beijing
Laykyun Sekkya, the world's third tallest statue at 116 meters (381 ft) depicting Gautama Buddha completed in Khatakan Taung, Myanmar
Joachim I Nestor, Prince-elector of Brandenburg (opposed Protestantism), born in Cölln, Margraviate of Brandenburg (d. 1535)
Sethus Calvisius, German composer, born in Gorsleben, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1615)
Johann Augustin Kobelius, German Baroque composer, and kapellmeister, born in Wählitz, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1731)
Franz Xaver Josef von Unertl, Bavarian politician, born in Munich (d. 1750)
Justus van Effen, Dutch writer (founded Holland Spectator), born in Utrecht (d. 1735)
Christoph Stoltzenberg, German composer, born in Wertheim am Main, Wertheim County, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1764)
Edward Hawke, 1st Baron Hawke, British naval officer, won naval victory in Battle of Quiberon Bay, born in London (d. 1781)
Willem van Haren, Dutch poet (Human Life), born in Leeuwarden (d. 1768)
Louis-Pierre Anquetil, French historian, wrote "A History of France" for Napoleon, born in Paris (d. 1808)
Eise Eisinga, Frisian amateur astronomer (built the oldest functioning planetarium in the world in his house at Franeker), born in Dronryp, Dutch Republic (d. 1828)
Vincenzo Lavigna, Italian composer, born in Altamura, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1836)
Friedrich Carl von Savigny, German jurist and legal scholar (historical school of jurisprudence), born in Frankfurt (d. 1861)
Catharina of Württemberg, Queen consort of Westphalia (1807-13) as wife of Jérôme Bonaparte, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1835)
Fyodor Petrovich Tolstoy, Russian painter (VP: Imperial Academy of Arts 1828-68), born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1873)
Karl August Varnhagen von Ense, German biographer, diplomat and soldier (Die Sterner), born in Düsseldorf (d. 1858)
Francisco Manuel da Silva, Brazilian composer, born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 1865)
John Henry Winder, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Somerset County, Maryland (d. 1865)
Johann Wenzel Kalliwoda [Jan Kalivoda], Bohemian violinist, composer, and court conductor, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1866)
John Henry Newman, British thologian (Tract 90), educator, poet (Dream of Gerontius), Anglican priest, Catholic priest, cardinal and saint, born in London, England (d. 1890)
George Douglas Ramsay, American Brevet Major general (Union Army), born in Dumfries, Virginia (d. 1882)
David Tod, US diplomat and politician (Gov-R-Ohio, 1861-63), born in Youngstown, Ohio (d. 1868)
Nicolo Gabrielli, Italian opera composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1891)
Ernest Meissonier, French painter and sculptor (depictions of Napoleon), born in Lyon, France (d. 1891)
Jose Zorrilla, Spanish poet and dramatist (El rey Loco), born in Valladolid, Spain (d. 1893)
Charles Scribner, American magazine and book publisher, born in New York City (d. 1871)
Johnson Hagood, American politician (Governor of South Carolina 1880-82) and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Barnwell, South Carolina (d. 1898)
Eduard Rappoldi, Austrian composer and violinist, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1903)
(Clement) Léo Delibes, French romantic composer (Coppélia; Sylvia; Le roi l'a dit; Lakmé), born in Saint-Germain-du-Val, France (d. 1891)
Charles-Marie Widor, French organist (Saint-Sulpice, 1870-1933), composer (10 organ symphonies), and professor (American Conservatory at Fontainebleau, 1921-34), born in Lyon, France (d. 1937)
Burner Matthews, American academic and critic who established study of theatre (NY Times), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1929)
Alice Freeman Palmer, American educator (President of Wesley College), born in Colesville, New York (d. 1902)
Hendrik Petrus Berlage, Dutch architect (Stock exchange Amsterdam), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1934)
Jules de Trooz, Belgium politician and Prime Minister of Belgium (1907), born in Leuven, Belgium (d. 1907)
Goscombe John, Welsh sculptor known for his public memorials "Port Sunlight War Memorial" and "The Response 1914", born in Cardiff (d. 1952)
Karel Matěj Čapek-Chod, Czech journalist, novelist and playwright, born in Domažlice, Austrian Empire (d. 1927)
Pierre de Bréville, French composer, author and professor (Une histoire du théâtre lyrique en France), born in Bar-le-Duc, Meuse (d. 1949)
John Haden Badley, English educator, founder of Bedales 1st UK co-ed boarding school, born in Dudley, England(d. 1967)[1]
Otto Hermann Kahn, German-American banker and patron of the arts, organized Metropolitan Opera Company, born in Mannheim, Baden, German Confederation (d. 1934)
Jeanne Calment, French supercentenarian, oldest verified person in history (122 years, 164 days), born in Arles, France (d. 1997)
Pyotr Konchalovsky, Russian painter, head of the Jack of Diamonds group, born in Sloviansk, Russia (d. 1956)
Totius [Jacob D. du Toit], South African poet and theologist, born in Paarl, Cape Colony (d. 1953)
Fritz Cortolezis, German conductor, opera director, and composer, born in Passau, Bavaria, Germany (d. 1934)
Mirra Richard [The Mother], French-born Indian spiritual leader (Sri Aurobindo), born in Paris, France (d. 1973)
Frank Orth, American actor (Boston Blackie, Brothers), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1962)
Waldemar Bonsels, German novelist, children's literature writer (Maya the Bee), and outspoken anti-semite, born in Ahrensburg German Empire (d. 1952)
Mick Commaille, South African cricket batsman (12 Tests , HS 47; Western Province, Northern Cape), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 1956)
Sacha Guitry, French actor and playwright (Deburan, The Story of a Cheat), born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1957)
Herbert J. Krapp, American theatre architect and designer responsible for almost half of current Broadway venues, born in New York City (d. 1973)
Savielly Tartakower, Austrian-Polish-French chess player, born in Rostov-on-Don, Russian Empire (d. 1956)
Clemence Dane [Winifred Ashton], British novelist and playwright (A Bill of Divorcement), born in Blackheath, England (d. 1965)
Willem Banning, Dutch theologist and sociologist (Religious Build Up), born in Makkum, Netherlands (d. 1971)
Felix Aylmer, British actor (Anastasia, Hamlet, Iron Duke, Macbeth, St Joan), born in Corsham, Wiltshire, England (d. 1979)
Andres Segovia, Spanish classical guitarist, born in Linares, Spain (d. 1987)
Vsevolod Pudovkin, Soviet film director and theorist (Mother, Deserter), born in Penza, Russia (d. 1953)
Henrik Dam, Danish biochemist who discovered vitamin K and won the 1943 Nobel Prize in Medicine, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1976)
Sylvain Poons, Dutch stage actor and singer (Blue Jackets), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1985)
Celia Lovsky, Austrian-American actress (Soylent Green, T'Pau-Star Trek), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1979)
Vasily Vainonen, Russian ballet dancer and choreographer (Gayaneh), born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1964)
Jeanne Aubert [Perrinot], French stage, silent and sound screen actress, and singer, born in Paris (d. 1988)
Madeleine Renaud, French actress and theater director (Plaisir), born in Paris, Ile-de-France (d. 1994)
Sydney Arnold, British comic actor (The Meaning of Life, Top Secret!, Miss Jones & Son), born in Westminster, London, England (d. 1993)
Arthur Nock, English-American historian (St Paul, history of religion), born in Portsmouth, England (d. 1963)
Bob Scholte, Dutch vocalist, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1983)
Anaïs Nin, French-Cuban writer (Delta of Venus), born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France (d. 1977)
Eric Fogg, English composer (The Golden Butterfly), conductor, and broadcaster, born in Manchester, England (d. 1939)
Fairfax M. Cone, American leading advertising executive (Foote, Cone and Belding), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1977)
Raymond Queneau [M Presle], French author (Un Enfant a Dit), born in La Havre, France (d. 1976)
Tom Yawkey, American Baseball HOF executive (owner Boston Red Sox 1933-76), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1976)
Armand Preud'homme, Belgian organist and composer (Kempenland), born in Peer, Belgium (d. 1986)
W. H. Auden [Wystan Hugh Auden], British-American poet (Age of Anxiety Pulitzer Prize 1948), born in York, England (d. 1973)
Neville Quinn, South African cricket fast bowler (12 Tests, 35 wickets), born in Tweefontein, South Africa (d. 1934)
George van Renesse, Dutch pianist and music director, born in Zaamslag, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Hans Erni, Swiss painter and sculptor, born in Lucerne, Switzerland (d. 2015)
Helen Octavia Dickens, American physician and gynaecologist (1st black women admitted to US College of Surgeons), born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 2001)
Al Sears, American jazz and R&B saxophonist (Duke Elllington Orchestra; Johnny Hodges - "Castle Rock"), born in Macomb, Illinois (d. 1990)
Carmine Galante, American Mafia chieftain of the Bonanno New York crime family, born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1979)[1]
Douglas Bader, British pilot and WWII flying ace during WWII, born in London, England (d. 1982)
Eddie Waring, British rugby league broadcaster (BBC 1958-81), born in Dewsbury, England (d. 1986)
Arline Judge, American actress (Girls in Chains, Mad Wednesday, Age of Consent), born in Bridgeport, Connecticut (d. 1974)
Nikita Magaloff, Georgian-Russian pianist (1st to record Chopin's complete works), born in St Petersburg, Russia (d. 1992)
Glenn M. Anderson, American politician (Rep-D-CA, 1969-93), born in Hawthorne, California (d. 1994)
Arnold Denker, American chess champion (1944-46), born in New York City (d. 2005)
Ann Sheridan, American actress (They Drive by Night; Dodge City; King's Row; Another World), born in Denton, Texas (d. 1967)
Godfrey Brown, British athlete (Olympic gold 4 x 400m, silver 400m 1936), born in Bankura, India (d. 1995)
Lucille Bremer, American actress and dancer (Meet Me In St. Louis; Ziegfeld Follies), born in Amsterdam, New York (d. 1996)

Tadd Dameron, American jazz pianist, composer ("If You Could See Me Now"; "Lady Bird"), arranger (Count Basie, Jimmie Lunceford, Dizzy Gillespie, Billy Eckstine) and bandleader, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1965)
Victor Marijnen, Dutch politician (Prime Minister of the Netherlands 1963-65), born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 1975)
Richard Whitcomb, American NASA aerodynamicist, invented the supercritical wing and devised the area rule, born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 2009)[1]
Uroš Krek, Slovenian composer, born in Ljubljana, the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (d. 2008)
Zvi Zeitlin, Russian-American classical violinist and music professor (Eastman School, 1967-2012), born in Dubroŭna, Russia (now Belarus) (d. 2012)
Al Fann, American actor (Alvin-He's the Mayor), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2018)
Jack Ramsay, American Hall of Fame basketball coach (Portland Trail Blazers, 1977 NBA Champions), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2014)
Sam Peckinpah, American film director (The Wild Bunch, Straw Dogs), born in Fresno, California (d. 1984)
Hans Andreus [Johan Wilhem van der Zant], Dutch children's author (Mr. Bumblemoose books), and poet (Muziek voor Kijkdieren), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1977)
Erma Bombeck, American humorist (The Grass is Always Greener over the Septic Tank), born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 1996)
Pierre Mercure, Canadian composer (Kaléidoscope), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1966)
Cecil Sandford, British motorcycle road racer (125cc world champion 1952; 250cc world champion 1957), born in Blockley, England (d. 2023)
Philip von Schantz, Swedish painter, born in Norrköping, Sweden (d. 1998)
Henry Kloss, American audio engineer (acoustic loudspeaker), born in Altoona, Pennsylvania (d. 2002)
James Beck, British actor (Dad's Army), born in Islington, London (d. 1973)
Elizabeth Adams, American Beverly Hills madame and mentor to Heidi Fleiss, born in Manila, Philippines (d. 1995)[1]
Leon Black, American college basketball coach (Texas Longhorns men's team 1967-76), born in Martins Mill, Texas (d. 2021)
Bob Rafelson, American Emmy Award-winning television producer (The Monkees), and film maker (Five Easy Pieces; Easy Rider; Head), born in New York City (d. 2022)
Gordon Forbes, South African tennis player and author (French Open mixed doubles [Darlene Hard] 1955), born in Burgersdorp, South Africa (d. 2020)
Rue McClanahan, American actress (Maude, Golden Girls), born in Healdton, Oklahoma (d. 2010)
Jean Pelletier, French Canadian politician (Chief of Staff to the Prime Minister, 1993–2001; Mayor of Quebec, 1977-89), born in Chicoutimi, Quebec (d. 2009)
Mark McManus, Scottish actor (Taggart; 2000 Weeks; Ned Kelly), born in Hamilton, South Lanarkshire, Scotland (d. 1994)
Barbara Jordan, American politician (Rep-D-Tx, 1972-78), born in Houston, Texas
Gary Lockwood, American actor (2001, Survival Zone, Lieutenant), born in Van Nuys, California
